key_points: A habitat is the natural place where organisms live
Ecosystems consist of biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components
Producers make their own food through photosynthesis
Consumers depend on other organisms for food
Decomposers break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients
Food chains show the transfer of energy between organisms
Food webs are interconnected food chains
Ecological balance is maintained through interactions among organisms
examples: Forest habitat: trees, animals, insects, soil and sunlight
Pond ecosystem: fish, frogs, algae, water and nutrients
Food chain example: Grass → Deer → Tiger
Decomposers example: fungi breaking down dead leaves
formulas_or_relationships: Food Chain: Producer → Primary Consumer → Secondary Consumer → Tertiary Consumer
Energy Flow: Sun → Plants → Herbivores → Carnivores
Nutrient Cycle: Dead organisms → Decomposers → Soil nutrients → Plants
exam_tips: Remember that producers form the base of every food chain
Distinguish clearly between biotic and abiotic components
Use diagrams when explaining food chains and ecosystems
Give examples of ecological relationships such as mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism
Explain the role of decomposers in recycling nutrients
